title: The Sultan’s False Hand
blurb: Ink runs red with secrets. A scribe’s hand, trained to steady the Sultan’s own words, trembles only when he recognizes the birthmark on his shoulder. One moonlit night. One missing page of gold. Seven weeks until the Sultan rides home—and the Grand Vizier’s knives are already sharp.
comparable: For fans of *The Gilded Wolves* by Roshani Chokshi, *The Shadow of the Wind* by Carlos Ruiz Zafón

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The scriptorium smelled of crushed gallnuts and burning lamp oil. Sun slanted through the arched windows, striping the marble floor in gold. Emir Effendi adjusted the position of his inkstone by a finger’s breadth, the way his father had taught him—never the same twice, so the chisel marks didn’t betray habit.

“Too careful,” muttered the boy beside him, smearing vermilion across a deed of tax remission. “A scribe should work like a man carving his name on a tombstone. Fast. Final.”

Effendi didn’t look up. His sleeve rode high enough to hide the crescent scar on his palm, a brand of shame no one in the guild was meant to see. The burn puckered like old fruit leather. He flexed his fingers beneath the table edge.

A pot tipped. Vermilion splashed across the ledger like a wound.

The apprentice yelped, clutching the overturned vessel. Ink puddled toward Effendi’s knee. He knelt without thinking, pressing his sleeve into the spill, blotter fashion.

Gold spread in a slow bloom.

He lifted the cloth. A dark stain bled through the linen.

Footsteps slowed nearby.

Ceylan Hatun stood three paces away, holding a tray of gessoed vellum. Her gaze dropped to his shoulder—the exposed fold of his tunic had pulled loose when he moved. A birthmark, pale as candle wax, glimmered in the lamplight.

A crescent moon.

Her breath caught. The tray listed. One sheet of vellum fluttered free, drifting to the floor like a fallen star.

Effendi’s hand shot up, catching the edge before it touched the marble.

Ceylan didn’t move. Her eyes locked on his scarred wrist, the way his fingers trembled—not from ink, not from apology, but from the effort to tuck his sleeve back into place.

She set the tray down. Her voice was level, but too light. “You’ve been hiding in this scriptorium longer than a moon hides in daylight.”

He stood. The vermilion stain darkened his cuff. “Shall I fetch a rag?”

“No need.” She didn’t blink. “I’ve seen the mark before.”

The words hung. Apprentices glanced their way, sensing weather shifting without knowing the storm’s name.

Ceylan turned toward the stacks. “Keep that sleeve tucked. Darker inks suit the royal lexicon.”

Her slippers whispered against the marble.

Effendi watched her retreat—her spine rigid, the knot of her hair tight as a siege rope. He exhaled through his nose. No one here knew about the prophecy, the brazier, the night his “father” had shoved him into flame and pronounced him dead. But she did. She had once illuminated love poems between Süleyman and his first wife—poems embroidered with hidden sequences. She could read a birthmark like cipher.

And now she held a secret that could unravel empires.

A gong clanged from the Divan’s outer chamber—end of the second watch. Lala Şahin appeared at the top of the marble staircase, her silver-threaded shawl catching the light like a banner. “Effendi. Your hand, child.”

She descended, her slippered feet silent on the stone.

He bowed slightly. “Mother.”

“You’re trembling.”

“Only the ink.” He curled his fingers into his palm. The burn site pulsed, a dull ache.

She reached for his wrist, but he flinched. The sleeve rode up again, exposing the scar.

Lala Şahin’s breath stilled. Her eyes flicked to his shoulder blade, then away. Too fast. Too guilty.

Effendi said nothing. He never did when secrets pressed close.

She patted his arm instead. “Come. The grand vizier requests your presence in the outer chamber. Some decree needs ‘refinement.’”

The apprentice who had spilled the ink gaped. “At the Divan, Effendi?”

Lala Şahin’s smile was tight. “He’s the best we have.”

Effendi followed her up the staircase, his sandals clicking against stone. At the landing, he glanced back once.

Ceylan wasn’t in the stacks anymore. But her vellum lay on the floor where it had fallen.

A single sheet, carrying the ghost of a royal cipher he had sketched in his youth. A cipher only she would recognize.

He bent to pick it up.

As his fingers brushed the parchment, a shadow shifted in the hallway’s far arch. A man in a black kaftan, his beard streaked with silver, watched through half-lidded eyes. He didn’t speak. Didn’t need to.

Effendi straightened, tucking the vellum into his sleeve.

The man turned and walked away.

The scent of bergamot and old regret lingered.

Chapter 2

The Divan’s outer chamber smelled of crushed lapis and old parchment. Sunlight slanted through the clerestory windows, striping the marble floor in gold. Emir Effendi stood at attention, ledger in hand, his posture a study in quiet endurance. Around him, scribes and officials shifted, their sandals whispering against polished stone.

The Grand Vizier arrived without announcement.

Silence fell like a dropped scroll. Damat Ibrahim Pasha swept into the chamber, his crimson kaftan whispering against the floor. His dark beard was meticulously trimmed, his expression unreadable. He carried a single rolled decree, its silver seal glinting in the light.

Effendi did not flinch.

“Master Effendi,” Ibrahim said, his voice soft as a blade sliding from its sheath. “A word.”

The scribes parted. Effendi stepped forward, his pulse steady, his palm damp beneath the linen. The burn scar throbbed, a dull reminder.

Ibrahim unrolled the decree without ceremony. The silver seal cracked. The parchment rustled like a whisper of dissent.

“Page three,” Ibrahim said. “Third line from the bottom.”

Effendi’s eyes flicked to the text. The decree was flawless. He had checked it himself, twice.

Ibrahim’s finger tapped the parchment. “‘By command of the Sultan, may Allah prolong his shadow, the fortress at Van shall be supplied with grain sufficient for two thousand men for the term of six months.’”

A pause. The room held its breath.

“You see the error,” Ibrahim said.

Effendi did not. The grain allocation was correct. The syntax was precise. The date—

He saw it then. A smudge beneath the word *six*. A dab of ochre, no larger than a freckle. A typo. His typo.

His stomach dropped.

Ibrahim’s lips curled. “You have wasted gold leaf.”

The chamber exhaled. Scribes exchanged glances. Effendi’s fingers tightened around the ledger. The burn on his palm flared.

“You will rectify this,” Ibrahim said. “Publicly.”

Effendi’s throat tightened. He knew what was coming.

Ibrahim crumpled the decree in one hand. The parchment crackled like a curse. He held the wad of gold-stamped parchment out to Effendi.

“Lick it clean,” he said. “Then you may return to your bench.”

The chamber stilled. A bead of sweat traced Effendi’s spine.

He knelt.

The parchment’s edge was rough against his lips. The ink tasted of copper and shame. He pressed his mouth to the crumpled gold, his tongue clean and precise as a surgical stroke. The decree’s edges bit into his skin.

Ibrahim leaned down. His breath was warm, laced with cloves.

“You thought ink could hide a bloodline?” he whispered. “The only thing royal about you is the stain on your tongue.”

Effendi’s jaw clenched. The taste of parchment and gold filled his mouth, metallic and bitter. He swallowed.

Around him, the scribes did not meet his eyes.

Ibrahim straightened. “Rise.”

Effendi obeyed, his legs steady beneath him. The parchment’s ghost clung to his lips. He turned toward the drafting tables, his ledger clutched tight.

Ibrahim called after him. “And Effendi?”

He paused.

“Next time, use your knife to scrape the error. No one likes a sloppy mouth.”

The chamber erupted in stifled laughter. Scribes bowed their heads, hiding smiles. Effendi walked to his bench, his steps measured, his face a mask.

He did not sit.

Instead, he opened his ledger to a blank page. His hand moved, steady and precise, recording every syllable, every humiliating breath. The words took shape, a cipher only he would understand. A ledger of shame, turned to a ledger of power.

Behind him, Ibrahim conferred with the Divan officials, his voice a low purr of satisfaction. The scribes returned to their work, their pens scratching like mice in the walls.

Effendi wrote until his wrist ached. The ink ran black as the future he would carve from this moment.

Then, without a word, he closed the ledger and slipped it into the sleeve of his robe.

The chamber emptied. The afternoon light shifted, striping the marble in gold once more.

Effendi did not look up. He did not flinch. He waited.

Chapter 3

The attic cell smelled of damp wool and cedar shavings. Effendi knelt on the rush mat, his knife pressed to the beam’s grain. The blade trembled once, just before it bit wood. He steadied his wrist by recalling the Qur’an’s ninety-ninth verse on witness and record—every stroke a truth, every cut a debt.

The door creaked.

Lala Şahin filled the frame, her shadow spilling across the floor. She held a clay cup of ayran, its surface trembling like her hands. The lamplight caught the silver threading in her headscarf, turning it to a river of mercury.

“You’ll catch a chill,” she said, voice rough as sanded olivewood.

Effendi didn’t look up. His fingers worked the knife’s edge along the beam’s curve, carving Ibrahim’s words in mirror-image script. The Grand Vizier’s voice still echoed in the Divan: *Use your knife to scrape the error. No one likes a sloppy mouth.*

“You always did learn too well,” Lala said. She stepped inside, the floorboards groaning beneath her. The cup tilted; a splash of white liquid stained the hem of her skirt.

Effendi set the knife down. He reached for the cup, steadying it before it could spill further. The ayran was warm, slightly sour on his tongue—mixed with a splash of last night’s rakı, she’d said once, when she thought he wasn’t listening.

“You’re drinking in the dark,” he said.

“And you’re carving confessions in a beam,” she replied. “We’re both sinners tonight.”

He exhaled through his nose. The knife lay between them like a judge’s blade. “Why are you here, Lala?”

She sat on the wooden chest that held his few stolen books—al-Kindi’s lost treatise on cipher, a volume of Hafiz, a repaired copy of *The Book of Kings* she’d smuggled from the palace library when he was twelve. The chest’s lock was broken. She’d done it herself.

“I had a dream,” she said. “You were standing in the Divan, but there was no parchment in your hand. Only a cup of ink.”

Effendi picked up the knife again. The beam’s surface was smooth now, the words settled into the wood like ink into crevices. He traced the last phrase with his fingertip—*the stain on your tongue*—and felt a twitch in his scar.

“Dreams don’t run the empire,” he said.

Lala set the cup on the floor. It rolled slightly, leaving a wet trail on the wood. “No. But men do. And you’re playing with fire, my little moon.”

He finally looked at her. The lamplight carved deep shadows beneath her eyes. She was older than he remembered. Or perhaps he’d forgotten how time passed when he wasn’t counting years.

“You gave them my name,” he said.

The words hung. The attic held them like a held breath.

Lala’s fingers twitched toward the cup. “Only to a man who called himself a friend. A man who swore he’d keep you safe.”

“And did he?”

She didn’t answer right away. When she did, the word came slow, heavy with something like regret. “No.”

Effendi placed the knife beside the cup. His palm rested on the beam; the carved words pressed against his flesh. “He knows.”

“He knows you’re dangerous,” Lala said. “He doesn’t know who you are.”

“He will.”

A gust of wind rattled the single shuttered window. Dust danced in the lamplight like fleeing souls. Effendi felt the cold seeping through the cracks in the beam—just as he’d felt it that night, the firelight licking at his skin while a man with a scholar’s fingers tossed him into the brazier and called it mercy.

Lala reached out. Her fingertip hovered over the carving, tracing the silver ink. “You were always clever. But clever boys get caught in clever traps.”

“I’m not a boy anymore.”

“No.” Her voice cracked. “You’re something else.”

Effendi stood. The knife was heavy in his hand. “You told me once that the empire is a book. Pages can be rewritten.”

“And who gets to turn the page?” she asked.

He didn’t answer. Instead, he took the cup from her and drank the rest of the ayran in one swallow. The liquor burned his throat—a small fire, nothing like the one that had branded his palm.

Lala’s eyes filled. One tear escaped, tracing the line of her cheek like a river finding its course. “I thought I was saving you.”

“You were saving yourself,” he said.

She flinched as if struck.

Effendi stepped closer. The knife was between them now, the blade catching the lamplight like a warning. “Tell me you didn’t do it for the guild. For the quiet life. For the ayran on cold nights.”

Lala swallowed. Her hand went to her headscarf, twisting the edge. “I did it for the boy who slept in the scriptorium every night with ink under his nails and poetry in his pockets. I did it for *my* little moon.”

Effendi leaned in. His breath stirred the hairs at her temple. “He’s gone.”

Silence.

Then, softly, from the darkness below, a boot scraped against stone. A cough. The murmur of a guard changing shift. The empire didn’t sleep, not even in attics.

Lala’s fingers tightened around the cup. “Effendi—”

“But you can still choose,” he said. “You can tell them *my* name again. Or you can tell them nothing.”

She looked at the carved beam. Then at the knife. Then at him.

Her voice dropped to a whisper. “They already know.”

Effendi didn’t move. “Who?”

“Ceylan.”

The name struck like a needle through parchment. His breath stopped.

Lala’s eyes filled again. “She came to the guild hall today. She asked for you. By name.”

The knife slipped from his fingers. It clattered against the cup, sending ayran splashing across the wood like blood.

Lala whispered, “I never told them your name.”

Effendi answered, “But you will.”

Chapter 4

The domed treasury office smelled of mint and fresh lacquer. Ibrahim Pasha stood centered beneath the golden chandelier, his crimson silk kaftan catching the light like a wound. Around him, foreign merchants in emerald and indigo rotated like planets, their rings flashing as they toasted with cups of sherbet.

A junior clerk cleared his throat from the arched doorway. His collar was damp with sweat. "Your Excellency, a disturbance in the Süleymaniye stacks."

Ibrahim didn’t turn. He adjusted the gold molar in his signet ring with his thumb. "A disturbance? Be precise."

"The night watch reports a scribe moving through restricted corridors. Claims the man was carrying a case of gold leaf."

One merchant, a Genoese with a scar across his cheek, frowned. "Gold leaf in the stacks? That’s a fire hazard."

Ibrahim laughed, deep and rich, like a man who had never worried about flames. "Our scribe is bold. Or foolish." He raised his voice slightly. "Send someone to retrieve him. Quietly."

The clerk bowed and exited, the marble floor swallowing his steps.

The vizier raised his cup. "Gentlemen, drink. The Sultan’s return is weeks away, and until then, the empire’s ledgers belong to us."

Glasses clinked. Laughter rose like steam from a fresh inkwell.

Ceylan kept her head low as she slipped through the treasury’s side door. She wore a borrowed merchant’s sash wrapped twice around her waist, the dye still wet. In her arms, two rolled folios pressed against her ribs—proof wrapped in linen.

A guard near the fountain shifted his halberd. "You’re not on the list."

She didn’t slow. "And you’re not paid to question women who walk like they belong here." She passed within an arm’s reach, letting the scent of rosewater and shellac trail behind her.

The guard exhaled through his nose but didn’t follow.

In the corridor beyond, the marble cooled her bare feet. She unrolled one folio just enough to confirm the cipher—thin lines of gold leaf spelling Süleyman’s private name in the margin. *The sultan’s handwriting. No one else could have written this.*

She rolled it tight and tucked it into her sash again.

A shadow filled the arch ahead.

Effendi leaned against the wall, his fingers tracing the edge of a parchment scroll. His sleeves were rolled to the elbow, revealing the crescent scar on his palm—a pale crescent against sun-browned skin.

Ceylan stopped.

His eyes lifted. Recognition flared, sharp and immediate. He didn’t smile. Didn’t speak.

"Did you follow me?" she asked.

"I follow no one," he said. "But I’ve been waiting for the moment you decided to walk through a door without permission."

She stepped closer. "You always did overestimate your own importance."

"Not as much as you overestimate your safety." He held up the scroll. "The Süleymaniye’s eastern gallery is locked after sunset. You broke the seal."

She watched his fingers. They were steady. Unafraid. "I didn’t come to discuss locks, Effendi."

His face flickered—just for a heartbeat. The name was a spark in dry tinder. "Then why are you here, Ceylan?"

"Because I found something." She pulled the rolled folio from her sash and held it out, weighted in her palm. "And you’re the only one who can read it."

He didn’t take it. "After four years? After you left without a word?"

"I left because you told me to." Her voice dropped. "You wrote me a letter. Said the cost of being seen with me was too high."

"That was different."

"Was it?" She pressed the folio against his chest. "Open it. Right now. Before Ibrahim’s spies find us both."

He exhaled through his nose. Then, slowly, he unrolled the sheet. The gold leaf caught the torchlight, turning the cipher into fire.

The merchants’ laughter echoed behind them, brittle as glass.

Ceylan watched his face. His jaw tightened. A muscle jumped beneath his ear.

"You see it," she said softly.

"Yes."

"Then you know what it means."

He rolled the folio back up. His fingers trembled just once. "It means we’re both dead before the Sultan’s caravan reaches Edirne."

"You could run."

"And leave you to face the Divan alone?" He almost laughed. "You always did think too highly of me."

She reached out, hesitated, then touched the parchment over his wrist. "I never stopped believing in you."

Effendi closed his hand around hers. His palm was warm. The scarred skin pressed against her knuckles like a brand.

Then he let go.

"We have less than an hour," he said. "The treasury guard changes at midnight. I know the route through the ventilation shafts."

Ceylan’s pulse jumped. "You still have access?"

"Access is different from safety." He turned, leading her toward a narrow stair. "Follow me. And stay silent. If you speak, we’re already dead."

She fell into step behind him.

The air grew thick with dust and old stone. Somewhere above, the vizier’s feast continued, glasses chiming like a false harmony.

Ceylan kept her eyes on Effendi’s back. The man she used to love. The man who had become a ghost.

They reached a spiral stair. He paused, listening.

Then, from below, footsteps echoed.

Effendi pressed Ceylan against the wall. His body blocked hers completely. She felt the rise and fall of his breath, smelled ink and sweat and something older—regret.

The footsteps grew louder. A guard hummed tunelessly.

Effendi didn’t move. Didn’t breathe.

Ceylan’s fingers curled into the stone. She felt the vibration of a boot on marble, the clink of a belt buckle.

Then silence.

The guard had stopped.

Effendi exhaled slowly. He turned his head just enough to whisper in her ear, his lips brushing her temple. "He’s waiting for us to move."

Ceylan nodded.

They didn’t.

The guard coughed. A boot scraped stone.

Then the footsteps resumed—walking away.

Effendi waited five more heartbeats. Then he took her hand and pulled her down the stair, two steps at a time.

Ceylan’s pulse roared in her ears.

They moved through darkness. Through danger. Through memory.

And as they descended, neither of them noticed the slip of parchment that fell from Effendi’s sleeve and fluttered unseen onto the marble floor—an old verse, half-burned, the edges singed black.

It bore Ibrahim Pasha’s monogram in the corner.

Chapter 5

The scriptorium’s marble floor felt colder than the treasury had. Effendi’s sandals left faint damp prints as he crossed the threshold, his sleeves still rolled to the elbows from the chase through the ventilation shafts. The master calligraphers stood in their ordered rows, their reed pens aligned like spear tips in a quiver. No one met his eyes.

Grand Vizier Damat Ibrahim Pasha stood beneath the central dome, his jeweled dagger catching the afternoon light like a firefly’s wing. He held a parchment rolled in his left hand, the imperial seal still warm. "Effendi," he said, stretching the name like a complaint. "You’re late."

Effendi stopped three paces away. His burn scar itched beneath its linen wrap. "Your Excellency’s summons arrived only this morning. I came as soon as the ink dried."

A dry chuckle rippled through the circle of masters. Ibrahim’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. "Always the precise scribe. But today we don’t need ink. We need obedience." He unfurled the parchment. "By order of the Divan, the scriptorium’s hierarchy is hereby reorganized. The old guild ranking is abolished. From this moment forward, authority flows from the treasury office."

One of the masters, an old man with ink-stained knuckles named Osman, stepped forward. "With respect, Your Excellency, the gold-to-ink ratios for the new decrees require precision not yet achieved. The ratios are—"

"Lost," Ibrahim finished. "Yes, I’ve heard. Which is why I’ve assigned a new head calligrapher—Ahmet Bey, lately of the Divan’s private office." He gestured to a man in a plain indigo kaftan who bowed stiffly. "He will establish new standards."

Ahmet Bey’s eyes flicked to Effendi, then away. His fingers were soft, unmarked by work.

Effendi watched the man’s wrists. No calluses, no scars. Not a scribe. A clerk. A ledger man.

Ibrahim’s gaze sharpened on Effendi. "You look troubled, Effendi. Do you doubt my judgment?"

Effendi kept his voice quiet. "I doubt the ink will hold, Your Excellency. The old ratios took a lifetime to perfect. Without them, the gold will flake. The decrees will crack. The empire’s words will bleed."

Ibrahim’s ringed fingers tightened on the parchment. "Then you’ll have to rediscover them."

A silence settled. The air smelled of crushed violets from the ceiling frescoes.

Osman spoke. "We’ve searched the archives. The old texts are—"

"Missing," Effendi said. "As I said." He met Ibrahim’s stare. "The technique is tied to a specific sequence of measurements. Without the exact proportions, the gold will fracture within a week."

Ibrahim’s smile returned, slow and deliberate. "Then we’ll do without gold. The empire saves coin, and the scriptorium learns resourcefulness."

Osman’s Adam’s apple bobbed. "Your Excellency, even basic gilt requires—"

"Enough." Ibrahim raised the parchment, rolled it tight. "The order stands. Ahmet Bey begins tomorrow. And Effendi—" He paused. "You are relieved of your editing duties. Effective immediately."

The masters stiffened. The air in the scriptorium cooled by a degree.

Effendi felt the burn on his palm throb. He bowed. "As you command."

Ibrahim stepped closer, close enough for Effendi to smell bergamot and old paper. "You don’t sound happy."

"I’m a scribe, Your Excellency. Happiness isn’t in the charter."

The vizier laughed, a single sharp sound. "No. But obedience is." He clapped Effendi’s shoulder, fingers pressing just hard enough to remind him of rank. "Go. Edit nothing. Speak to no one. And if I hear you’ve whispered to the masters about ratios…" He left the sentence hanging.

Effendi said nothing.

Ibrahim turned to the assembled masters, his voice booming. "Continue your work. The empire’s decrees must be flawless."

The masters bowed their heads in unison.

Effendi walked to the door. His sandals made no sound.

Outside, in the courtyard where boys ran with ink pots and frustrated apprentices hurled reed pens into the fountain, he paused beneath the arched colonnade. The marble was warm now, heated by sun. He pressed his palm against the cool stone.

The burn flared.

A shadow fell across the ground beside him.

"Effendi."

He didn’t turn. "I was relieved of duty."

"I heard." Ceylan Hatun stood in the doorway, her sleeves rolled to the wrist despite the heat. A streak of ultramarine stained her forearm. "They took your edit stamp."

"Ahmet Bey has his own."

She stepped closer. "You look like you’ve been flayed."

"Not the first time." He finally turned. "What do you want, Ceylan?"

She held up a scrap of paper, the edges singed black. "This fell from your sleeve in the treasury corridor. Ibrahim’s monogram is in the corner."

Effendi’s fingers curled. "A verse. I was carrying a letter."

"Funny. The paper was warm." She tucked it into her sash. "You’re playing a dangerous game."

"I’m the only one playing it."

She stepped into the light. Her eyes were bright, assessing. "You left me in the ventilation shafts."

"I saved your life."

"Then why did you pull me into this?"

"Because you stole Süleyman’s cipher," he said. "And you brought it to me."

She didn’t flinch. "I wanted to know if the boy we lost was really you."

"And now you do."

She reached out, hesitated, then brushed her fingers over his wrist. Her touch was light, almost accidental. "You’re not a ghost anymore, Effendi. But you’re not safe either."

He pulled his hand away. "Neither are you."

She smiled, a quick sharp thing. "I never was."

A boy in a tattered kaftan darted past, nearly colliding with them. He skidded to a stop, breathless. "Effendi Effendi! Mistress Şahin sent me. Says you’re to come at once. The Vizier’s men are in the Süleymaniye stacks—"

Effendi’s stomach dropped. "What men?"

"Guards. Two of them. With a warrant for a manuscript theft."

Ceylan’s breath caught.

Effendi looked at her. "They’re here for you."

She didn’t answer. Her eyes went to the scriptorium’s arched window, where Ibrahim’s laughter still echoed faintly inside.

Effendi grabbed her wrist. "Run."

She pulled free, but not before he saw the fear in her eyes—a flash, then gone, replaced by something colder. "I don’t run."

"Then you’ll be arrested."

She adjusted the sash over her ribs. "Let them try."

He wanted to shake her. Wanted to drag her into the shadows. But the courtyard was open. The sky was clear.

A shout echoed from the Süleymaniye gate.

Ceylan’s head snapped up. "That’s my cue."

She was gone before he could stop her, slipping between the colonnade pillars like smoke.

Effendi stood alone, the sun hot on his neck, the burn on his palm pulsating like a second heartbeat.

A guard rounded the corner, halberd glinting.

Effendi didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

The guard’s eyes flicked from him to the scriptorium door. Then he marched past, boots crunching on fallen reed pens.

Effendi waited until the man disappeared inside.

Then he walked to the fountain, cupped his hands, and drank.

The water tasted of copper and regret.

Chapter 6

The Black Quill’s air smelled of spilled rakı and old parchment. The tavern squatted in a cul-de-sac beneath the Süleymaniye’s eastern wall, its door a splintered relic reinforced with iron bands. Effendi entered without pause, the hinges groaning like a man out of joint.

Behind the counter, Ömer the tavern keeper wiped a wineglass with a rag the color of dried blood. His left hand twitched—an old burn, too. He didn’t look up. “You’re late.”

Effendi set two silver akçe on the counter. No plate, no cup. “I brought the child with me.”

Ömer’s fingers stilled. He lifted one coin, bit it, nodded. “That boy’s got a mouth like a magpie.”

“He didn’t speak on the way.”

“Good.” Ömer slid the coins into his pouch without counting. “What’s the message?”

“Ceylan Hatun. Tomorrow night. Süleymaniye’s midnight prayer service. Alone.”

Ömer exhaled through his nose, a sound like a bull about to charge. “You know she’s being hunted.”

“I know.”

“And you’re walking her into the lion’s den.”

“I’m walking her into the mosque library. Where the guards won’t follow.”

Ömer wiped the glass again. “She won’t come.”

“She will.”

“Why?”

Effendi didn’t answer. He turned toward the door, then paused. “The schoolboy who delivered the scroll to the Divan’s stables—he told the guards Ceylan took Süleyman’s cipher. Ibrahim’s men will be watching her.”

Ömer’s glass shattered in his hand.

The shards tinkled onto the counter like ice. Blood welled between his fingers. He didn’t flinch. “You set a trap.”

Effendi watched the trickle of red. “I set a rendezvous.”

Ömer grabbed a rag, wrapped it around his palm. “She’ll see it.”

“She’ll see the trap. But she’ll come anyway.” He stepped closer, voice low. “Because she loves the boy who was lost. And the man who found him.”

Ömer’s jaw tightened. “And if she doesn’t?”

“Then the trap still works. Ibrahim’s men will take her. And the cipher will burn before they can unravel it.”

Ömer stared at him, then gave a single nod. “Get out.”

Effendi left the coins where they lay, still sticky with spilled wine.

Outside, the street narrowed into a fissure between walls. A child crouched in the shadows, knees drawn to his chest, eyes wide and black.

“It’s done,” Effendi said.

The boy didn’t move.

Effendi crouched. “You did well.”

The child’s fingers clutched a scrap of parchment. He held it out without a word.

Effendi took it. The ink was still wet. Two words, in Ceylan’s hand: *Trust the verse.*

He tucked the paper into his sleeve, stood, and walked.

The child darted after him, bare feet slapping the cobblestones. “Will they catch her?”

Effendi didn’t slow. “She’ll catch them first.”

---

That night, beneath a moon fat as a stolen melon, Ceylan turned the corner into the Süleymaniye’s forecourt. The mosque loomed, its domes silvered by starlight, its courtyard empty save for the flicker of a single lantern by the ablutions fountain.

She wore a man’s kaftan, indigo dyed to hide her usual cuffs of gold-threaded silk. The cipher was sewn into the hem, a secret weight against her ankle. She’d memorized the poem, but the pages were proof. And proof could be burned.

A shadow detached from the arcade.

She didn’t jump. “If you’re here to arrest me, say so. I haven’t eaten all day.”

Effendi stepped into the lantern light. His sleeves were rolled, his hands ink-stained as always. “I’m not here to arrest you.”

“Then why the tavern? Why the coded summons?”

He didn’t answer. Instead, he turned and walked toward the mosque’s inner gate, his pace unhurried, as if they were scholars arriving for an evening study.

She followed.

The gate guards didn’t look up. They knew Effendi. They didn’t know her.

Inside, the mosque was hushed, the air thick with the scent of beeswax and old stone. The library stacks rose like silent sentinels, their shelves groaning under illuminated tomes and uncut quires of vellum.

Ceylan’s pulse quickened. “You lure me here under false pretenses, then refuse to explain?”

Effendi stopped at the base of the central aisle. “I lure you here because Ibrahim’s men are watching the scriptorium. They expect you to run. They don’t expect you to walk into the lion’s den.”

She crossed her arms. “You’re the lion.”

“No.” He turned to face her. “I’m the bait.”

She laughed, a sharp, incredulous sound. “You think I’m the fish?”

“You stole Süleyman’s cipher. You hid it. You brought it to me. You think I don’t know why?”

Her fingers twitched toward her sash. “You think you know everything.”

“I know the cipher is incomplete. Two pages are missing. The ones with the birth cipher.”

Her breath hitched. She didn’t speak.

Effendi stepped closer. “I know the poem ‘Clouds Over the Golden Horn’—the one you illuminated for Hürrem—contains a sequence of numbers. A birthmark cipher. A moon-shaped mark on the left shoulder blade.”

Her face went still.

“You saw it,” he said. “In the ventilation shafts. When we were children. Before the fire.”

She recoiled. “That’s impossible.”

“It’s a birthmark shaped like a crescent moon. Identical to Süleyman’s.”

She backed up, her boots thudding against the marble. “You’re lying.”

“I’m not.”

“Then why didn’t you say anything then? When we were children? Why let me think you were just a scribe’s foundling?”

“Because I was.”

“Bullshit.”

He took another step. “You think I wanted to be the lost prince? You think I wanted to be hunted? I was a boy thrown into a brazier to fake my death. I survived. I became a scribe. I edited decrees. I wrote verse. I waited. And now—”

A floorboard creaked.

Both turned.

At the far end of the aisle, beneath the shelf that held the imperial Qur’an, a guard stood motionless. His halberd’s blade caught the lamplight.

Ceylan’s hand flew to her sash. “Shit.”

Effendi didn’t move. “He’s not here for you.”

“Then who—”

The guard spoke. “Ceylan Hatun. By order of the Divan, you are under arrest for the theft of an illuminated manuscript.”

She froze.

Effendi’s voice was calm. “The manuscript isn’t here.”

The guard didn’t blink. “I have a warrant. You are to come with me.”

Ceylan’s fingers closed around the cipher in her hem. “And if I refuse?”

“Then I will be forced to detain you.”

She glanced at Effendi. “You set me up.”

“No.” His voice was quiet. “I set a trap. And you walked right into it.”

The guard shifted his halberd. “Now, Mistress.”

Ceylan’s eyes locked onto Effendi’s. “Where’s the fire exit?”

He pointed to the stacks behind him. “Through the illuminated folios. The third shelf has a loose panel. Behind it is a ventilation shaft. It leads to the Süleymaniye’s eastern courtyard.”

She didn’t hesitate. She bolted.

The guard lunged.

Effendi moved.

He tackled the guard from the side, driving him into the shelf of illuminated tomes. Ink pots shattered. Gold leaf rained like autumn.

Ceylan didn’t look back.

She ducked into the stacks, elbows and knees flashing as she wriggled into the narrow gap. The loose panel groaned as she shoved it aside.

Effendi and the guard grappled on the floor, the man’s halberd scraping marble.

Then a shout from the doorway.

Another guard.

Effendi’s breath left him in a rush. He rolled off the first guard, grabbed a fallen folio, and hurled it like a discus.

The ink-slicked page struck the second guard square in the chest.

The man staggered, arms windmilling.

Effendi didn’t wait. He turned and ran for the stacks, boots thudding.

Behind him, the guards cursed, crashed into shelves, sent folios tumbling like wounded birds.

Ceylan’s voice echoed from the darkness. “Effendi!”

He skidded to a halt at the ventilation shaft’s mouth. She was halfway through, her kaftan snagged on a broken lath.

He dropped to his knees, grabbed the fabric, and yanked.

The kaftan tore.

She tumbled into his arms, her breath hot against his neck.

For a heartbeat, they were still.

Then the guards’ shouts grew louder.

Effendi pushed her toward the shaft. “Go!”

She hesitated, then scrambled inside, her legs kicking like a trapped doe’s.

He turned.

The two guards emerged from the aisle, halberds leveled.

Effendi raised his hands.

The guards advanced.

Then a third figure stepped into the lamplight.

A boy.

The street urchin from the Black Quill, eyes wide, a rusted nail in his hand.

He swung.

The nail struck the nearest guard’s wrist.

The man howled, dropping his halberd.

Effendi didn’t think. He lunged, seized the fallen halberd, and swung.

The shaft struck the second guard across the temple.

The man crumpled.

The first guard clutched his wrist, blood dripping onto the marble.

Effendi stood over him, halberd trembling in his grip. “Run.”

The boy didn’t need to be told twice. He turned and bolted, his footsteps echoing into the night.

The remaining guard looked at his fallen comrades, then at Effendi, then turned and fled.

Silence settled.

Effendi dropped the halberd. It clattered onto the marble.

Then he heard it.

A whisper of movement.

From the ventilation shaft.

A hand emerged, slender and ink-stained.

Ceylan’s voice came, muffled. “You came back for me.”

He didn’t answer.

He reached down and took her hand.

And pulled.

Chapter 7

The marble scriptorium smelled of crushed walnut shells and rosewater. A dozen master calligraphers sat in the tiered stalls, their reed pens scuttling like crabs across fresh vellum. At the central table, Lala Şahin’s abacus beads clicked too fast—a slow, deliberate counterpoint to the usual rhythm.

Effendi adjusted the hem of his indigo caftan, the linen still stiff from the library chase two nights before. His left palm ached where the burn scar pulled against the fabric. He kept his hands on the table, palms down.

An apprentice stood suddenly. “Effendi, do you have any insight into the failed decree?”

The pens stuttered. A master calligrapher coughed. Lala Şahin’s fingers stilled on the abacus—the beads hung suspended like a held breath.

Effendi rose. The room tilted slightly, the way it always did when he stood after too much ink that night. He didn’t look at Lala Şahin.

“Respectfully,” he said, “the decree wasn’t flawed. The fault lies with the parchment’s preparation. The quire absorbed moisture from the draft near the treasury’s humidification basin. The ink bled because the surface tension failed.”

Silence. Then a ripple of murmurs. Some nodded; others shifted in their seats.

Lala Şahin exhaled through her nose, a sound like a bowstring released. Her abacus resumed its slow, hypnotic click.

A master calligrapher cleared his throat. “Then we must reprove the treasury’s storage conditions.”

“Of course,” Effendi said. “It’s simply a matter of air circulation.”

The apprentice flushed, gripping his reed pen too tight. “I meant—about the wording. The phrasing of the Sultan’s title.”

Effendi turned. “The title was correct. The ink was not.”

The apprentice’s pen snapped in half.

A tiny crack, like a dry twig. The nib fell to the floor. The pen’s shaft rolled under the nearest stall. From under the table, a single gold-leaf flake glinted.

It spun once, twice, then settled.

No one noticed but Effendi.

He stepped forward, careful not to disturb the flake. He picked up the broken pen, snapped the halves together. “It’s an old tool. Poorly balanced.”

Lala Şahin’s abacus beads slipped. One flew off, clattering onto the marble.

The room exhaled.

Effendi didn’t pick up the bead.

He placed the broken pen on the central table and sat.

The apprentice’s face burned. He mumbled an apology and fled.

The masters returned to their work. The scuttle of pens resumed. The scent of crushed walnut shells deepened.

Lala Şahin’s voice was soft, barely audible. “My little moon, you dance too close to the flame.”

Effendi didn’t answer.

He dipped his reed pen into the inkwell. The black pooled, viscous and deep. He drew a single, perfect alif on a scrap of vellum.

Then he folded it twice and tucked it into his sleeve.

The abacus beads clicked.

The masters wrote.

The gold-leaf flake lay untouched on the marble floor, catching the afternoon light like a secret.

Chapter 8

The Black Quill reeked of spiced wine and old paper. The air above the candlelit tables shimmered with heat and the static charge of too many whispered words. Ceylan Hatun moved between the booths like a shadow with a heartbeat, her fingers wrapped tight around a copper dirham pressed into her palm by the tavern owner.

She hadn’t slept since the scriptorium chase. Not since Effendi had looked at her with eyes that knew too much—and not since the spies of Damat Ibrahim Pasha had dragged her from the Süleymaniye library, their ink-stained cuffs leaving gray smudges on her sleeves.

The tavern owner wiped a tankard with a rag that had seen better days. “You’re out of your depth, Hatun. They’re not just looking for a thief—they want a name.”

Ceylan set the coin on the counter. “I gave them nothing.”

He leaned in. The candle between them bent like a bow. “They asked about the girl who knew the cipher. The one who slips in and out of the palace like smoke. They asked about *him* too.”

She didn’t flinch. “Who?”

“The quiet scribe. The one who edits like a ghost.” He nodded toward the back room. “He’s been here before. Always after dark.”

Ceylan turned. A figure sat alone in the furthest booth, shoulders hunched over a cup of something dark and bitter. She knew the shape of him—the long neck, the way his wrists bent when he wrote. She knew the rhythm of his breath.

She walked toward him.

Effendi didn’t look up. His fingers traced the rim of the cup, leaving faint prints in the condensation.

She stopped at the foot of the table. “You shouldn’t be here.”

He finally raised his gaze. Not to her face—for a moment, lower. At her throat. Then slowly up, like a man counting steps in the dark. “Neither should you.”

The tavern owner brought another cup without being asked. He set it down with a thud that rattled the table.

Ceylan sat. Not across from him. Beside. Close enough that their sleeves brushed when she reached for the cup. She didn’t let him look away.

“They took Süleyman’s cipher,” she said. “I didn’t steal it. It was *gone* before I even touched the shelf.”

Effendi lifted the cup to his lips. Took a slow sip. Put it down. “I know.”

She exhaled through her nose. Not anger. Relief. “Then you know why I ran.”

“Because you’re afraid of what they’ll do when they realize you know too much.” He tapped the table once. Twice. “Or because you’re afraid they’ll find out what you don’t.”

She froze. “What does that mean?”

“It means the pages weren’t stolen,” he said, voice low. “They were hidden.”

Ceylan’s breath hitched. The candle between them guttered. “Hidden by whom?”

Effendi didn’t answer. He reached into his caftan and placed a single sheet on the table. A scrap. Torn. The edges uneven, as if ripped from a book. At the top, in faded gold lettering, the header of the Süleymaniye archive.

Ceylan’s fingers hovered over the page. She didn’t touch it.

“This was in the treasury ledger,” Effendi said. “Bound inside the spine. Like a secret.”

She recognized the script—hers. Or nearly hers. The illumination style was unmistakable: delicate strokes, lapis and ochre, the practiced curve of a hand she’d once loved.

She turned the page over.

On the back, in a different hand, ink still wet, were two words:

*He lives.*

Ceylan’s pulse stuttered. “You’re saying—”

“I’m saying the cipher wasn’t stolen,” Effendi said. “It was *left* there. For someone to find.”

The tavern owner appeared at their shoulder. “I don’t want to know. I don’t. But you should. Ibrahim’s men just left. And they weren’t asking questions anymore.”

Ceylan looked up. The door swung shut behind two men in long, dust-colored cloaks. Their boots left faint imprints in the sawdust on the floor.

Effendi folded the page slowly, carefully, like a letter meant to be burned. “They’re not spies anymore. They’re hunters.”

She caught his wrist as he tucked the page away. His pulse jumped under her fingers—fast, like a trapped bird.

“You knew,” she said. “You knew they’d come for me.”

“I knew they’d come for *us*,” he corrected.

The door banged open again. This time, the cold night air rushed in, carrying the clatter of hooves on cobblestone. A mounted patrol passed outside, their torches throwing flickering shadows across the tavern walls.

Ceylan leaned closer. “Then why did you send me—?”

He silenced her with a finger to his lips. Then he nodded toward the back door.

She followed his gaze.

A man in a merchant’s robe stood just inside, eyes scanning the room. His fingers drummed against a ledger tucked under his arm. The leather cover was too fine for a merchant. The ink stain on his cuff too dark.

Effendi’s voice was barely a whisper. “That’s not a buyer. That’s a collector.”

Ceylan’s stomach twisted. “Of what?”

“Of secrets.”

The man’s gaze flicked toward their booth. Met Effendi’s. Held.

Then he smiled—a thin, practiced curve of lips—and walked straight toward the tavern owner.

Ceylan stood. Effendi didn’t.

The man stopped at their table. He didn’t sit. Didn’t ask. Just set the ledger down between them.

The cover was slit open. The spine bled.

Inside, a single page. And on that page, in fresh, bold strokes:

*Damat Ibrahim Pasha*

*By the authority of His Imperial Majesty, all claims of succession—real or imagined—are hereby nullified. Any person found in possession of proof of such claims will be executed without trial.*

Ceylan’s breath stopped.

Effendi’s face didn’t change. But his left hand, hidden under the table, clenched into a fist. A burn scar pulled tight across his palm.

The man closed the ledger with a snap.

“You have until the Sultan returns,” he said. “Six weeks. The ledger already contains a list.”

He walked out.

The door swung shut.

Ceylan sat back down. Her hands trembled.

Effendi exhaled through his teeth. “We have less time than I thought.”

She wanted to reach for him. To take his hand. To press her palm to his scar and ask what he remembered of the night he was thrown into the brazier. But the space between them felt wider than the sea.

Instead, she pointed at the ledger. “That’s not a list of claimants. That’s a confession.”

Effendi looked at her. Really looked. For the first time in years, she saw not the scribe. Not the prince. Not the ghost.

She saw the boy who once wrote her love poems in cipher.

“It’s also a blood signature,” he said. “Father’s ink. My handwriting.”

The tavern owner came over, wiping his hands on his apron. “You want me to burn it?”

Effendi shook his head.

Ceylan grabbed the ledger. The paper was still damp. The ink hadn’t dried.

It was still *bleeding*.

Chapter 9

The scriptorium courtyard at dusk smelled of damp marble and crushed herbs, the scent rulers used to freshen the air when they walked among their slaves and scholars. Lala Şahin’s sandals pattered against the tiles, her headscarf pulled low despite the absence of sun. She carried a folded paper in her right hand—pressed flat like a promise she couldn’t keep.

Emir Effendi sat on the low marble bench beneath the lemon tree, a sheaf of imperial decrees resting on his knees. He didn’t look up when the shadow of her body fell across his page.

“You’re still correcting Ahmedi’s work,” Lala said. “Even after he was named head calligrapher.”

Effendi’s reed pen didn’t pause. “And you’re still treating me like a child.”

She sat beside him, close enough that her sleeve touched his. The folded paper crackled between them like dry leaves.

“I brought an olive branch,” she said.

He dipped his pen in the inkwell. “I only accept branches that bear fruit.”

Lala exhaled through her nose. Not a laugh. Not anger. Something older. “You always did love parables, my little moon.”

Effendi finally looked at her. Not at her face—at the paper in her hand.

“Then you know what’s coming.”

She unfolded it. The edges were damp where her tears had touched. A confession. Words scribbled in haste, in a script Lala Şahin had taught a boy twenty years ago.

*His name is Emir.*

*He burns like the moon.*

Lala tapped the page. “Sign this. Say it was a mistake. A lapse of judgment. Admit error. I will speak for you. Ibrahim will relent.”

Effendi set his pen down. The ink blotted on the last page he’d touched.

“I no longer make errors,” he said.

The breeze shifted. Leaves scraped across stone. Lala’s hands shook. Not just the confession in her grip—her entire body trembled, like a woman standing on the edge of a bridge she didn’t know was there.

“I didn’t want to,” she said. “But they asked. And I was drunk. And you were gone. And the boy—” Her voice cracked. She swallowed. “The boy in the brazier, the one they swore was dead—he had a name. And I gave it to them.”

Effendi stared at the confession. Not the words. The stains. The way her tears had curled the ink, making the letters bleed like old wounds.

“You think this saves me,” he said.

“I think it buys you time.”

He stood. The decrees slipped from his lap, scattering across the tiles like fallen stars. He picked one up. A minor correction to a tax edict. A single word changed from *all* to *none*.

“Time for what?” he asked.

Lala clutched the confession to her chest. “To leave. Before Ibrahim realizes what you’ve already done.”

Effendi crushed the decree in his fist. Not in anger. In calculation.

“And what have I done?”

“You’ve infiltrated the treasury. You’ve altered the ledgers. You’ve left a trail—”

“A trail of what?”

“Proof.”

She said it too quickly. Too desperate. Like a gamble.

Effendi stepped closer. Close enough to see the gray in her hair, the way her knuckles whitened around the confession.

“You think I’m gathering proof against Ibrahim,” he said.

“Aren’t you?”

He reached out. Not to take the confession. To touch her wrist. Gently. Like a son might touch a mother.

“Tell me, Lala,” he said. “If I were gathering proof… would I leave it in the treasury where he could find it?”

Her breath hitched.

Effendi released her. Turned toward the scriptorium doors. The golden afternoon light cut across his shoulders, painting him in fire.

“Go home,” he said. “Burn that paper. Forget you ever wrote it.”

Lala didn’t move. The confession trembled in her grip. “And if I can’t?”

He didn’t look back.

“Then you’re already too late.”

She opened her mouth. Closed it. The confession slipped from her fingers, skittering across the stone like a dying bird. She didn’t bend to pick it up.

Effendi walked into the scriptorium without a word. The doors swung shut behind him.

Lala stayed. The scriptorium swallowed her breath. The confession lay unclaimed in the dusk, its ink drying, its secrets already slipping into the marble cracks.

Then she knelt. Picked it up. Pressed it to her forehead.

And wept.

Chapter 10

The Black Quill’s cellar reeked of spilled wine and candle wax at the base of the stone steps. Effendi moved through the dark like he was following a line of thread no one else could see. Three men sat at a corner table, their coins stacked between them, their breath thick with the gold they thought they were gambling.

Ahmet Bey, the new head calligrapher, leaned forward as the dice rolled. His sleeve brushed the ledger open on the table—ink still wet, the imperial seal still smudged at the corner. Effendi didn’t pause. He didn’t acknowledge the spice merchant from Bursa who had once been his ally in the scriptorium, now sipping murky raki with his sleeves rolled up.

A lantern flickered. The coin stack trembled.

“You’re late,” Ahmet said, not looking up. “Or early. Depends on whether you’re here to lose or win.”

Effendi placed a single sheet on the table between them. The paper bore the treasury’s watermark, the numbers aligned in neat columns, but the totals were wrong. The imperial revenue from this quarter was half what it should have been.

“Lose,” Effendi said.

Ahmet’s thumb traced the altered ledger line. “That’s not how the game is played.”

“It is now.”

The other men stilled. The dice stopped rolling.

Effendi tapped the paper. “These are the accounts Ibrahim Pasha submitted to the Divan last week. Notice anything?”

Ahmet’s jaw tightened. “You altered them.”

“Embezzlement is easier when the ledgers lie.”

A man across the table reached for a dagger. Effendi didn’t flinch. He pulled a second sheet from his sleeve—this one signed by the chief treasurer himself, confessing the flaw before it was discovered. The man hesitated. The blade stayed sheathed.

Ahmet exhaled through his nose. “You’re not playing to win. You’re changing the rules.”

“Someone has to.”

Effendi gathered the coins into his palm. The silver was cool, heavy. He slid one across the table to Ahmet.

“Tell the guild the correction was yours,” he said. “Say you found the error before the Divan did. Say you acted to protect the empire’s honor.”

Ahmet stared at the coin. Then at the ledger. Then at Effendi’s face.

“And if I don’t?”

“Then you’re part of the corruption.”

The cellar went quiet. The lantern’s light flickered over the stone walls, over the shelves of dusty wine bottles and rolled parchments.

Ahmet took the coin. Slid the altered ledger toward himself. “What do you want me to say?”

“That the treasury is flawed. That Ibrahim Pasha’s accounts are unreliable. That the Sultan must return to audit the books himself.”

Ahmet’s fingers flexed around the coin. “And when he does?”

Effendi smiled. Just once. Not a grin. Not a threat. Something precise.

“He’ll see the truth.”

---

The Süleymaniye Mosque library smelled of aged paper and myrrh incense. The air was thick enough to taste, layered with centuries of whispered prayers and scholar’s sighs. Effendi stood at the edge of the reading hall, watching the arched windows throw rectangles of gold across the marble floor.

Ceylan Hatun knelt at the central table, her hair bound in a tight braid, her fingers stained with cinnabar and gold leaf. She didn’t look up as he approached. A half-bound Qur’an lay open before her, its pages glinting under the lamplight.

“You’re touching something you shouldn’t,” she said.

“Like you did with Süleyman’s cipher?”

She finally looked at him. Her eyes were tired. Not angry. Not afraid. Just weary, like a woman who had spent too many nights tracing secrets that weren’t hers to keep.

“I didn’t steal it,” she said. “I hid it.”

Effendi stepped closer. The floor creaked under his sandals.

“And now?”

She dipped her brush into the inkwell. “Now I’m wondering if any of us ever had a choice.”

He leaned over the table. The open page showed Süleyman’s love poem to Hürrem, the words illuminated in deep blues and scarlet reds. Effendi’s finger traced the crescent moon beside the Sultan’s signature.

“You left a cipher in the binding,” he said. “A trail leading here. To the archive.”

Ceylan’s hand stilled. “The archive is sealed.”

“Not for long.”

She set the brush down. “You’re planning to open it.”

“Yes.”

“Why?”

“Because the proof isn’t in the cipher. It’s in the birthmark.”

A monk in the shadows shifted, but Ceylan didn’t look away.

“You think I haven’t checked?” she said. “I’ve seen the scars. The burns. The knife marks. I’ve traced every imperfection. There’s nothing on your shoulder shaped like a crescent.”

Effendi rolled up the sleeve of his robe. The burn scar on his palm was faint in the lamplight, but the one on his inner arm was newer—a thin red line, half-healed.

Ceylan didn’t touch it. But her breath caught.

“That’s not—” She swallowed. “That’s not what I meant.”

Effendi let his sleeve fall. “I know.”

The library was silent. The monk had left. The only sound was the whisper of Ceylan’s breath and the distant call to prayer from the mosque minaret.

She picked up a scrap of parchment. A single line, written in cipher. The same one Effendi had used on his first imperial decree.

“This isn’t your hand,” she said.

“No.”

“Whose is it?”

Effendi didn’t answer. He reached into his belt and placed three things on the table between them.

A silver key. A folded decree. A ledger page with Ibrahim Pasha’s seal stamped in red wax.

Ceylan stared at them.

“You forged the imperial Qur’an,” she said.

“And the succession decree inside.”

Her fingers trembled as she picked up the key. It was warm from his body.

“This opens the archive.”

“Yes.”

She lifted the deed. The wax broke under her touch. The Sultan’s signature was unmistakable.

Ceylan’s voice was barely a whisper.

“You’re not just a scribe.”

Effendi looked at the ledger. The numbers were false. The accounts were ruined. The treasury was exposed.

“I’m what’s left,” he said.

Ceylan set the key down. Then the decree. Then the ledger.

“You’re asking me to bind the empire,” she said.

“No. I’m asking you to bind the truth.”

She stood. The chair scraped against the marble.

“And if I refuse?”

Effendi didn’t answer. He didn’t have to. The archive door was just beyond the stacks. The lock was new. The key was in her hand.

Ceylan looked at the courtyard beyond the windows. The sun hung low over the Bosporus, painting the water in gold and fire.

“I’ll do it,” she said. “But I want to see him first.”

Effendi knew who she meant.

“He’s not ready.”

“He doesn’t have to be. Neither do I.”

Effendi picked up the key. Slid it into her palm. His fingers brushed hers. Not a touch. A contract.

“Tonight,” he said. “Before the caravan reaches the city gate.”

Ceylan closed her fist around the key.

“Then I’ll bring the poem,” she said.

Effendi almost smiled.

Almost.

He turned toward the archive door.

“You always do.”

Chapter 11

The Süleymaniye’s treasury vault was a cavern of iron and silence. Gold dinars spilled from cracked chests like gilded blood, their edges blunted from years of handling. Ibrahim Pasha stood in the center, his boots pressing into scattered coins, his breath a slow hiss through clenched teeth.

The treasurer’s ledger lay open on a brass stand. Its pages were signed, sealed, and inked with confessions—each one scrawled in a trembling hand. The Grand Vizier picked up a single sheet, the one that named him the architect of the empire’s slow bleed.

“You told me the flaw was discovered,” he said to no one.

A man in a striped turban, his face puffed from a night of questioning, knelt in the corner. Blood crusted his split lip. He didn’t look up.

Ibrahim crushed the paper in his fist. “You lied.”

“No, my lord.” The man swallowed. “I *remembered* the truth after the third finger.”

Ibrahim’s shadow stretched long across the gold. He didn’t strike the man. He didn’t need to. The vault already held the sounds of his rage—the creak of the iron door, the whisper of parchment, the distant clink of a loose coin sliding across marble.

Outside, a guard coughed. Ibrahim turned toward the sound. The door swung open.

A captain stood there, his uniform stiff with starch, his face gray.

“My lord,” the captain said, voice steady. “Lala Şahin has been taken.”

Ibrahim didn’t move. “The wet-nurse?”

“She was found in her quarters with a ledger of the prince’s movements. It’s in Effendi’s cipher.”

Ibrahim exhaled through his nose. The name settled into the vault like a curse. Effendi. The scribe with the quiet hands and the dangerous eyes. The man who had waited twenty years to rise from ash.

“Where is she now?”

“Being questioned in the Divan’s chambers.”

Ibrahim stepped over a chest of sequins. “And the prince?”

The captain hesitated. “He was seen leaving the scriptorium an hour ago. Alone.”

Ibrahim’s fingers tightened around the confession page. “Find him.”

The captain bowed. The door closed.

Silence returned. The vault swallowed the echo of footsteps.

Ibrahim looked down at the ledger again. The numbers bled together. Revenues diverted. Soldiers paid to stand idle. A pattern of theft so vast it would take a decade to unravel—if the empire lasted that long.

He dropped the page.

It fluttered like a dying bird onto the gold.
